title: Numbers & Time
shortSummary: Count, tell the time, and talk about days using Korea's two number systems.
culturalNote: Korean uses two complete number systems, and choosing the right one is the single biggest hurdle for learners. Native Korean numbers (하나, 둘, 셋 - hana, dul, set) are used for the hour, for counting people and objects, and for ages in casual speech. Sino-Korean numbers (일, 이, 삼 - il, i, sam), which come from Chinese, are used for minutes, money, dates, phone numbers, and floors of a building. Telling the time mixes both at once: the hour is native Korean and the minutes are Sino-Korean, so 3:20 is 세 시 이십 분 (se si isip bun). Koreans also count age in a way that has recently changed: the traditional Korean age system added a year at birth and another every New Year, but the country officially moved to international age in 2023, so it is now common to clarify which you mean.

keyPhrases.context: Native numbers for hours and counting
Sino numbers for minutes and money
Asking the day of the week

grammar.explanation: Native Korean numbers count hours, people, and things, while Sino-Korean numbers count minutes, money, dates, and phone numbers. You must learn which context takes which system, because using the wrong one sounds clearly off to a native speaker.
grammar.commonMistake: Using Sino numbers for the hour; the hour is always native, as in 세 시 (se si).

grammar.rule: Telling the time: 시 and 분
grammar.explanation: The hour takes 시 (si) with a native number and the minutes take 분 (bun) with a Sino number. So 3:20 is 세 시 이십 분 (se si isip bun), mixing both systems in one phrase.

examples.native: 다섯 시 삼십 분 (daseot si samsip bun)

grammar.commonMistake: Saying 삼 시 instead of 세 시 for three o'clock; the hour needs the native number.

dialogue.setup: Jisoo asks a passer-by for the time before a meeting.

lines.speaker: 지수
lines.english: Excuse me, what time is it now?

lines.speaker: 지수
lines.english: Thank you. What day is it today?

lines.speaker: 지수
lines.english: The meeting starts at four o'clock.

lines.speaker: 행인
lines.english: Then you have a little time.

lines.speaker: 지수
lines.english: Yes, that's a relief. Thank you!

quiz.question: How do you ask 'What time is it now?' in Korean?
quiz.explanation: 지금 몇 시예요? (jigeum myeot siyeyo) asks the current time.

quiz.question: Which number system is used for the minutes when telling the time?
quiz.explanation: Minutes (분) take Sino-Korean numbers, while the hour (시) takes native numbers.

quiz.question: 세 시 이십 ___. (the counter for minutes)
quiz.explanation: 분 (bun) is the counter for minutes, so 3:20 is 세 시 이십 분.

quiz.question: Which says 'It's Monday'?
quiz.explanation: 월요일 (woryoil) is Monday; the others are Friday, Sunday, and Saturday.

quiz.question: Arrange: 분이에요 / 세 시 / 이십 (it's three twenty)
quiz.explanation: Hour first then minutes: 세 시 이십 분이에요.
